canto_title: The Fourth Heaven and the Catalog of Luminous Souls

scenes.summary: The poet describes the eternal, ineffable love shared between the Father and the Son.
Dante invites the reader to lift their gaze toward the celestial motion.
The poet explains how the oblique paths of the planets are necessary for the stability of the world.
Dante tells the reader to sit back and enjoy the feast of the text provided.
The poet describes the sun as the primary instrument of nature that measures time.
Dante reflects on how he only noticed the upward motion after he had already begun to ascend.
The poet describes Beatrice's rapid and profound perception of divine goodness.
The light within the sun is described as a pure, internal luminosity rather than mere color.
Dante admits that human art and intellect cannot fully imagine the divine sight.
The poet notes that human imagination is too low to reach such celestial heights.
The arrival in the fourth sphere of the heavens is introduced.
Beatrice instructs Dante to give thanks to the Sun of the angels for his ascent.
Dante describes his immediate and total surrender to Beatrice's words.
Beatrice's joyful reaction to Dante's devotion divides his mind with its brilliance.
Dante observes many bright souls forming a crown of light.
The poet compares the celestial lights to a ring of light around the sun.
The poet describes the joys of the heavenly court and the waiting for news.
The bright souls revolve around the center like stars around a pole.
The lights appear like women standing silently to listen to the music of the spheres.
St. Thomas Aquinas begins speaking about the multiplicative power of divine grace.
Thomas asks Dante to identify the souls that make up the heavenly garland.
Thomas identifies himself as part of the holy flock of St. Dominic.

scenes.scene_name: The Presence of Albert the Great
scenes.summary: Thomas identifies his teacher, Albert of Cologne, as being beside him.
Thomas directs Dante to look around the entire circle of the blessed.
The poet identifies the luminous presence of the canonist Gratian.
The poet identifies St. Peter Chrysologus and his charitable legacy.
The poet describes a particularly brilliant fifth light with profound wisdom.
The poet identifies a light that saw deep into the angelic nature.
The poet identifies the small light of St. Augustine.
The poet instructs the reader to move from light to light through the praise.
The poet speaks of the holy soul that perceives the truth of the world.
The poet describes the resting place of the saint's earthly body.
The poet identifies the burning spirits of Isidore, Bede, and Richard.
The poet identifies the light of Siger of Brabant and his use of syllogism.
The poet uses the metaphor of a chiming clock to describe the movement of the heavenly spheres.
The poet concludes by describing the glorious, rhythmic motion of the celestial rotation.
